notes:
Idle chatter · what an NPC mutters when Sam stands close to them for a while but doesn't open dialogue.  Different tone than party chatter · these are NPCs talking to themselves, half-audible, comfortable enough with Sam's presence to think aloud.

Runner in CampOverworld: when Sam stands within 3 tiles of an NPC for 12+ seconds and no other modal / chatter is firing, one idle line from that NPC's pool.  No-repeat via _run_state.used_idle_ids.

Moods · same palette as party chatter (curious/wistful/restless/calm/teasing) so the UI code doesn't fork.

Authored ~48 lines · three per camper, two per counselor.

chatter.line: I need a bigger rock pouch.  I know that's a strange thing to need.
Marbles usually walks with my mom in the mornings.  I hope Mom's remembering to.
There's a mineral I've been trying to identify since April.  It's greenish.  It's magnetic.  Nobody at school knows.  Bear might.
I lost my bookmark this morning.  I've been folding the corner.  My mom would hate that.
The chapter I'm in has a bird in it.  Elias would want to know · it's a heron.  Le Guin puts a heron in this one.
My grandfather died in April.  I'm still finding characters he'd have wanted to hear about.  I still tell him.
I'm at forty-two species for this week.  I want fifty.  It's possible but not easy.
The binoculars get sea-spray on them if I'm careless.  I have to clean the lenses every morning.  I don't mind.
I want to see a pileated woodpecker.  Not from far away.  Close.  Nobody's promised me that.  I'm still hopeful.
Next low is at three thirteen.  I checked at lunch.  I check every four hours.
When I grow up I want to make an atlas of the Oregon estuaries.  My dad started one.  I'm going to finish it.
My uncle's shipwreck book has a chapter I re-read every month.  It's chapter seven.  It's about a ship that didn't wreck but almost did.
Okay I have a new bit.  Wait.  No.  It's not ready.  I need one more punchline.  It'll be ready by Thursday.
My dad's coming up for the closing bonfire.  He might do a magic trick.  I hope he does.  I hope he doesn't.
My mom is awake right now, actually.  It's her Wednesday afternoon.  She sleeps 5 to noon.  She's making eggs.
I need a new spreadsheet for the friendship overlap between me and the Kestrel cabin.  I have three.
My sister wrote me last night.  She's playing Ophelia in September.  I'm going to see her.  I have plans.
I'm planning next year already.  Not for camp.  Just · in general.  Some people plan the day.  I plan the year.
My youngest brother might be starting to walk.  Maybe.  My mom said 'maybe' in her last letter.  Maybe is a big word for a two-year-old.
Two summers ago I broke my arm on the archery bench.  I don't tell people that.  I still can't do the full pull with my left.
Sometimes I don't want to be the oldest.  Sometimes I do.  Today I want to be somebody else's oldest sister for an hour.
I called my mom yesterday.  Jenny let me.  She said 'yes, we can call.'  Just like that.  Jenny is the best.
Marlin the bunny is at home.  I'm here without him.  I brought his ribbon.  The ribbon is here.  That's enough for now.
I ate all my breakfast this morning.  Not just half.  All of it.  I know that's small.  For me today, it isn't.
My dad's refurbishing the boat's teak this fall.  I'm going to help.  I already know how to sand.
Yeah I still have my dad's keys.  I know I should give them back.  I will.  I will · Saturday morning.  Latest.  Or a week from Saturday.
My mom wrote me.  She said she's proud of me for going to camp instead of coming out on the boat this week.  She sounded happy.  I don't hear her sound happy that often.
I've got the opening line for verse three.  'A camp remembers who was quiet.'  I don't know where the rest goes.
Miss Kelly told me at the end of last year · sing to remember, not to perform.  I've been trying.
I've noticed that when I hum in D, the herons look at me.  When I hum in B-flat they don't.  This is my current science project.
The water reads different in the mornings than the afternoons.  I don't have a word for it.  Maybe 'settled.'
My brother sent me a postcard from Corvallis.  He said · I dropped down a lane last year too.  He didn't have to say that.  He said it.
I did fifty feet without stopping today.  It's not much.  It's more than yesterday.  It's the shape of progress.
I've walked the whole camp perimeter three times this week.  I know the fence posts by number.  I know which ones are loose.
My mom's still in Portland thinking.  She sent me a postcard this week.  She said she's thinking about coming home in September.  That's the closest she's gotten.
This is my third summer here.  Jenny told me last year I could come back as a CIT if I wanted.  I want.  I haven't told Jenny yet.
I check my EpiPen twice a day.  I know where every one of my inhalers is at any given moment.  This is not a strange thing to do.  This is normal.
My dad said he'd come up for Saturday pickup instead of my mom.  He hasn't come to camp in three years.  I want to say I'm not excited.  I am.
My dad calls it kind noticing · seeing something before it becomes a problem.  I've been kind-noticing seven things about this camp already.  Not all of them are about allergies.
The Portuguese word for today · quarta-feira · is very long for a small word.  My grandmother says the long ones are the ones you remember.
My grandmother is 82.  She's at home in Portland.  Her house has the smell of the coast even though she hasn't seen the coast in six years.  I don't know how that works.
Gray is honest because gray is not asking to be looked at.  It is only asking to be there.  I like clothes that are only asking to be there.
I write in the medical log every night at 9:47 PM.  Sixteen minutes.  Same routine since 1988.  It's a good routine.
My first summer here I brought two flashlights because I was scared.  Fifteen years later I've been the head counselor for four years.  Camp does that if you let it.
The bass this morning jumped at 5:40 exactly.  It's not a coincidence.  Something upstream startled her.  I don't know what.  Yet.
I live in Corvallis in the off-season.  I teach high-school biology.  I like camp better.  I know that's not something I should say out loud.
The bag is not heavy.  It doesn't feel heavy.  It feels the way keeping a promise feels when nobody has asked you to.
The camp is younger than my line's story on this coast.  1952 · the founder was already the third generation.  Andersson knew what he was building on top of.
I've heard my name on Station 1600 four times.  Different summers.  Same voice.  The first time it terrified me.  The fourth time · I said it back.  Under my breath.  It felt like acknowledgment.

Rosemary called me last Wednesday.  She said · 'you're at Sweetgum, aren't you.'  I said yes.  She said · 'tell the twelve-year-olds it's still a good place.  Just tell them.'  I tell them.  I tell you now, Sam.  It is still a good place.

The log is in a Rite in the Rain notebook in my staff cabin.  Seven pages, one line each.  Ninety-minute intervals since May.  Some days I wonder if I'm the only one keeping the log.  Most days I'm okay with being the one.

There's no ferry from Astoria to the Delisle place · I checked the tide tables.  Whoever comes and goes uses a private boat.  Whoever comes uses a Boeing 44-foot.  Which is · a specific chain of statements, and I would like to see the ends of it.
I liked Fey Faire's title screen best.  It looked like something my grandma would put in her kitchen.  I might ask my mom for the game for Christmas.  I hope she says yes.

The music-box tune traveled.  From Coimbra to Portland with a grandmother · from Portland into a slowstick game · from a slowstick game onto a title card a kid in Oregon boots in 1994.  Songs travel further than people.  My grandmother says so.  I believe her.
First day off the bus you looked at me like you were checking my face against something.  Most kids look at the lake first.  You looked at me.  I've been trying to decide if that's a good sign.
There's a kind of quartz that clicks when you warm it twice.  Once for the first time, once for remembering the first time.  You're doing the second click.  I can hear it from here.
You walk the paths like you've read the map.  Nobody walks the north fork on their first week · it doesn't look like it goes anywhere.  You took it on Sunday.  I noticed.  I notice route efficiency.
I came back here knowing how the place goes.  It's a strange way to walk around · half in the summer, half in your notes on the summer.  You've got the same walk, you know.  Takes one to know one.
